comment_text:
His explanations are incomplete, there are many causes and allergies are just one of them, though it's the easiest to diagnose.

It can be a sign of a problem with the immune system, which is harder to diagnose. I've never found the cause of mine, tested negative for allergies. 

If I had to mention 2 specific problems people miss, it would be celiac disease and alphagal syndrome. 

Celiac is the autoimmune disease where you can't have gluten, and it doesn't trigger an allergy test.

Alphagal is an allergy to a sugar molecule in mammal meat that you get after a tickbite. What's unique about it is that you won't react immediately, which makes it hard to diagnose.

comment_text: I'm 35, think it started when I was around 28, no allergies for 28 years and suddenly those blisterlike horribly itchy patches. Especially on hands, wrists, ankles, feet, elbowpits. All allergy tests negative... Been taking antihistamine pills since then, first I could last 3 days with one pill and had to keep increasing the dosage to 1 to 2 pills per day. Now, finally, after 7 years I know what causes it. Self diagnosed cuz doctors couldn't give me a cause. It's called SIBO, leaky gut etc, all connected with your gut. It makes sense cuz I've been eating unhealthy my whole life and been over 30 BMI for many many years. 3 months ago I went low carb and started intermittment fasting. Went from 90 to 80kg so far and to 1 pill per 3 days. I know high carb diet will trigger the itch cuz it has also something to do with insuline resistance. I'm off beta blockers for migraines and also quit the pil cuz if I'm correct it tempers with certain vitamine absorbtion. Only pill I take now is anti histamine 2 to 3 times a week. Major improvement!

Im a black South African male in my late 20s, by including Black im not starting any race issues, i love people of all color and race, please dont attack🤧
So this started in 2016, i took a shower only to find that the water was lukewarm, ive never really had issues before so i was calm about it, lowkey mad cause i love me a hot shower.

It happens again when we had a blackout and the water was cold😭started to notice a pattern, from then on id rather not shower unless its hot water, even in hot days, i need like 10 minutes of shower time to completely soak my skin to be okay.

comment_text: I got hives from my dogs flea medication and started taking benadryl. It got worse and worse till my face was so swollen I could only see out of 1 eye. All that to say that sometimes it can be compounded allergies from another source so consider everything your body comes in contact with when trying to identify a source.

But also, there are SO many thing you can have regular allergic reactions to in your home and narrowing down and illiminating things can be very hard and sometimes it can be a random thing you wouldnt think of.

And you can suddenly develop allegies to things you used your whole life. 
My gran was a nail tech and after 20 years randomly became allergic to acrylic and had to leave the industry.

My friend is allergic to water. Showering and some drinks make her break out. 

Im allergic to fake lavender scents. Certain mop chemicals, candles, fabric softeners, deodorant, etc make me break our on my face. Super fun to visit a friends house, sit on their couch and start itching. 

I have a coworker who gets stress hives, so thats also a thing.

I have the same issue (Hives). First time i felt it during winters in 2015. I was feeling itchy suddenly during night for a month. Then suddenly it stopped.
Then again in 2018 it started. Initially i was not taking any medicine for around 4-6 months. I used to feel itchy suddenly on my back, then on thigh, then shoulder then on my ribs. Its like someone is pinching with needle very fast her and there at the same time. My body feels hot and red marks shows up in my skin. The. After some time it calms down and no red marks nothing showing.

Cut to 2025 today, I am suffering from it. I showed to doctors and did the course of Levocitrizine for months. But it didnt stop.

Now i am living with it. I have Citrizine tablets (Rs 18 for one set) with me always. I break that medicine in 3 pieces and eat one piece every second day. So on an average I take one medicine of Citrizine every 5th day.

I feel there is no solution for this problem.

comment_text:
I got spontaneous chronic urticaria with face swelling as well as the vocal box swelling where it would ruin my voice. I was completely COVERED in big hives. My feet neck ears face, fingers everywhere. I saw several doctors for help. Nothing helped. They gave me presidone 4 in the morning and drop up to 1 a day for 14 days. As soon as I went to 3 the hives came back. They couldn’t give me steroid cream because it covered my whole body. They said I had to try several different type of pills before insurance would approve the shot that she mentioned. I didn’t want to be a genuine pig. This went on from feb to end of August none stop. Customers were scared of my face because of hives nobody want to take money during transactions. I was afraid to sleep because of the throat swelling. I changed everything hypoallergenic. I ended up going to the ER because my skin was so tender I was crying I hurt so bad. Everything that touched my skin made me cry. I felt the pain in my bones especially my wrists. The ER said they don’t normally take those cases as it’s not urgent. I told them it felt urgent and that none of the doctors were helping me.
So they gave me liquid Benadryl & a liquid steroid iv drip. I had a bad reaction to the steroid like I had been set on fire screaming and crying my arm went paralyzed & blue lips from my heart rate being super high from the pain of the IV meds. The doctor felt horrible for me because of how severe the hives were when I went in I’m talking all over. It took 5 hours of the iv to get most of the hives to go. But when I went home not all were gone it took about 24 hours..
I take 4 Zyrtec every morning as I was to continue to keep them away since I never knew what caused them. I can go about 3 days without if I am tight on money to get more but start to get itchy by 3 days. My hives weren’t normally itchy except when they first start up, after the first couple days they hurt. It took an ER visit for IV to get them to go away and for the doctor to take my suffering seriously. The rest of the doctors said “it’ll go away on its own”. Shame on them. I saw about 10 doctors from feb to the end of August. Have a lot of bills to pay for help I never received. It’s off brand Zyrtec but it needs the same ingredient if you take it to help manage. Also all my blood work for thyroid and inflammation and vitamins were all normal. Very weird

I also have hives (urticaria).
Earlier, I didn’t know what hives actually were, why they happen, or how they develop in the body. But when the redness increased and I noticed that whenever I scratched my skin or even drew a line with my fingernail on any part of my body, hives would immediately appear, I realized something was wrong. I was feeling very uncomfortable, so I visited a dermatologist.
After examining me, the doctor diagnosed it as hives and prescribed Desloratadine 5 mg. He explained that hives do not go away quickly if treatment is delayed. Recovery largely depends on how early you recognize the symptoms and consult a dermatologist—if treated within the initial months, recovery is usually much faster. In my case, I went late, so he advised me to take Desloratadine 5 mg for one month initially and mentioned that I might need to continue it for up to one year.
I can honestly say that since I started taking the tablet, my condition has been under control. However, if I stop the medicine, the hives come back again. I’ve been taking these tablets for the last four months, and I still don’t know exactly when this will completely go away.
After the Epstein files were released, many people started saying that COVID-19 was deliberately created. I watched multiple videos where it was claimed that the pandemic was intentionally designed so that people would become more vulnerable to diseases like heart attacks, kidney failure, asthma, high blood pressure, hives, urticaria, etc., and then antidotes or treatments could be sold for profit. According to these claims, powerful and wealthy people have disrupted the entire human life cycle worldwide for their own benefit.
Based on my personal experience, I would suggest Desloratadine 5 mg — one tablet in the morning and one in the evening, after lunch and dinner. It helped me maintain my condition.
Thank you.

Benadryl or any over the counter don’t help nor do anti itch creams. I hate my life a d want to cut my skin off… if only I didn’t feel pain. I feel they will never get better. They’ve cause me sever depression. I feel so unattractive & ugly. It’s worse enough I have ADHD that makes me dwell on negativeness. I believe they are caused by stress & no amount of trying not to stress out doesn’t work. My dad started when he stressed about money cause I haven’t been able to find work. So he pays my bills & rent. Because of my PTSD I’m always stressed about someone yelling out me.  These damn hives are never going to go away. I just want to die, I’m tired of being miserable 😭

comment_text: I am 22 years old and Every night since I was 5 years old I have broken out in hives. I am currently suffering so bad I am losing sleep as this issue has progressed worse. My skin flares up and gets intensely itchy and wakes me out my sleep. I scratch myself until I bleed and When I wake up in the morning the issue disappears like it never happened. So I nick named it “ghost rash”. I had to take photos to prove to my doctors I am not crazy and they diagnosed me with eczema but that was far from the truth. I have regular eczema on my legs but these hives are not eczema. I’ve taken benedryl so much throughout my life that I am now intolerant to the active ingredient. It gives me brain fog, irritability, massive headaches and overall instability physically and emotionally. I literally have no idea what else to do because my doctors have diagnosed me with different things and given me different treatment options yet none of them have ever worked. I’ve even tried changing my diet. Still no luck. So I am at the point now I am ready to hop up out of my skin.

comment_text: I’m sitting here and scratching my swollen spots on my legs and feet. I don’t want to wait another couple of weeks until I have met the requirements chronic urticaria to get proper testing (blood test) but I know doctors can only do their job. I’ve visited a doctor two times already and the first one said it was an allergy the second I explained my condition. That doctor prescribed me some medicine, loratadine for 10 days and panafcortelone for about a week. The same itchy red spots came back after maybe 5-7 days. The next doctor performed a closer examination at the spots and concluded that it was hives. They both gave me medicines which I felt sedated whilst taking. Maybe it is the protein in the foods I’m eating that my immune system is perceiving as “foreign intruders”....? Overall, I feel like more testing should be done if the patient is having pretty bad experiences. I heard him say that this is only temporary, and I can’t wait for it to be over!

comment_text: I got hives on my hands from washing my hands too often in hot water. Then the scratching started making some minor little spots spread. Then my diet made that worse as I love sauerkraut, and garlic almost every day. Six weeks of hell before I figured it all out. I stopped all of that and added Vit D, Omega 3 and Tudca bile salts supplements and it is finally going away. Make sure you are regular and quit all sugar, citrus and fermented foods for a while. If you experience itching, gently apply some watered-down apple cider vinegar to the spots. Celery juice helped my inflammation. Whatever you do, NEVER scratch them. Although some times external things can trigger these like hot water or stress in my case, it is usually from my understanding an internal problem first just ready for the right circumstances to unload on you.

comment_text:
I have it too. I first had it in 2017-2018 - it started with angioedema, and then I just had the hives. Very itchy, driving me crazy. I had allergy tests done, but they came back clear. The urticaria lasted for about a year, and then just went away. This year (2022) I got it again, all of a sudden. I suspect it might be a psychosomatic reaction to stress, although food definitely has an influence. 

I'm managing it with antihistamines, especially fexofenadine is good, but has to be taken in larger doses than normally prescribed. I take 180mg X2 a day. I'm avoiding all seafood and all red meat, as well as gluten, fermented foods, mature cheese etc. And also chocolate. And I'm trying not to eat too much of anything. And I drink loads of water. That way it doesn't get too bad. Also apparently vit D deficiency can worsen hives so it's good to check that. Maybe this can help someone.

Just to add, the antihistamines alone don't prevent the hives in my case, so if I don't watch the diet, I still get hives even on antihistamines. But they calm the itch a bit, without them my body would be so itchy I would not be able to sleep at night. But if I take antihistamines and keep the diet, and drink water - that can stop the hives from appearing.

comment_text:
For everyone who developed this out of nowhere and even tested negative for allergy test and are suffering with no idea why, I fortunately i have it diagnosed properly, it maybe an auto immune disease where the immune system is attacking itself. Antihistamines work but it won't help in the long term and there are treatments like wbc therapy but even they have less chances of working. comment_text:
Coming from someone who had CSU for about 2–3 years: for those going through their first episode of CSU, it’s important to know that this phase can last anywhere from 2–5 years, depending on the individual. I’ve already been through my first episode, which lasted about 2½ to 3 years, and it resolved on its own. I was then in remission for about a year.

It’s common that once someone experiences their first episode and enters remission, they may never have CSU again. That said, relapse is possible. One relapse is fairly common, while multiple relapses are much more rare.

I have Type IIb CSU, which is autoimmune-related, and I went into a relapse a few weeks ago. The encouraging part is that relapses typically do not last as long as the first episode.

It’s also important to understand that CSU is not a lifelong condition, unlike other autoimmune diseases such as lupus or rheumatoid arthritis. In many cases, it resolves on its own over time. However, many people receive treatment before it naturally resolves—such as injections like Xolair, which calm the immune system, suppress symptoms, and may even help “reset” immune responses.

Because of this, many people are unaware that CSU can resolve naturally, since treatment often controls symptoms before that point. However “Natural remission” takes time, so some people choose not to wait it out, which is completely understandable.

I’ve had this from nose to toes for over 2 years now.  Full body coverage with intense itch even in the scalp.  Instead of being present 24/7 like it was for 1st year it now cycles up and down and is tolerable. Changing jobs to something low impact/stress has helped immensely along with a new antihistamine called Betahistine.  
If I sweat, I break out. If it’s hot, sunny, cold, if I get sick, or get tired it’s a big breakout.  So managing it means getting decent rest, avoiding nsaids, staying out of sun and avoiding cold climates, and avoiding foods that are attributed to inflammation. As for exercise, I walk which isn’t a high sweat activity but no more weight lifting or running on treadmill.

comment_text: I recently discovered I'm allergic to canned beverages (namely aluminum!) For the longest time, I thought food allergens were the cause. I do have some food allergies, but regarding cans... the acidity of the beverage will corrode the inner lining of the can. Long-term exposure to the metal toxin (or even acute toxicity) will cause severe itching or dry/chapped skin especially on extremities & joints. If you've been drinking soda, sparkling H2O, beer from aluminum cans, I urge you to stop for up to 3 weeks. See if the itching & dryness goes away. Also if there's anyone you know, young or old, with eczema that they haven't found a way to control... but they have a habit of drinking sodas or beer from cans, try to get them off that for a duration. It's literally changed my life, & over the course of two weeks, I've seen my body completely change. There haven't been enough studies done on this, but I'm convinced that aluminum allergy doesn't simply involve instances of contact dermatitis. We ingest aluminum on a DAILY BASIS. Canned drinks are heavily consumed & over storage time the aluminum content increases. Give it a try.

comment_text: I am allergic to laundry detergents and fabric softeners. They give me wicked hives and the itch is absolute torcher. I now use a small amount of Tide Free to get my clothes clean and then I put my clothes through another "full" wash cycle, with no detergent to get most of the residual chemicals out and of course no fabric softeners or dryer sheets. This helped my skin by about 80%. Also, no one sharing the dryer should use fabric softener sheets as it will contaminate the dryer. Latex allergy are common so if your waistband area gets hives this may be a cause. Double wash all, bedding in hot water once a week for sure. This will help with dust mite allergies which can cause allergies when mites, feces, and the dead bodies of mites are inhaled. Many over the counter moisturizing creams only made me worse. Double washing really helped me, give it a try, but make sure to wash all clothes that way every time, it takes me more time and money do to laundry but its critical for me. I hope this advice can help someone else. Also, one last thing, soft drinks including diet drinks give me bad hives, so I cut them out completely.

Something in it, whether it was supposed to be there or not, caused me to breakout EVERYWHERE. It took less than 15 minutes for me to look like I’d walked right into a poison ivy bush and just rolled around in it lol. comment_text: About two months ago I did Yoga in my back yard. Just hands and feed touched the grass. I got a little itchy so I washed up, no problem. Took Benadryl and that calmed it but three days later it was back. Patches about two by one inch would well up, itch like crazy, calm down, then in another completely different area another would well up. It was crazy. Lasted about four weeks! I did three days of Benadryl at full dose.(I usually only need half a dose for a 24 hrs period) and it calmed it. Hydrocortisone anti itch helped  too.

comment_text: After a  Hep B vaccine and an HPV vaccine, and weekly allergy shots, my body just said nope, and my lower half had nickel-sized hives for about 6 weeks. At 44, I had never had a reaction to a vaccine before. The allergist put me on massive amounts of antihistamines (cetirizine 2pills twice daily and montelukast nightly) and an acid reducer (famotidine). They helped to decrease itching, but did not stop the actual hives. I started a weekly Xolair injection about a year ago, and it's been a game-changer. No bad side effects (for me) aside from injection site discomfort. The hives have not returned. I'm still on massive amounts of antihistamines for now. The only drawbacks of Xolair is the potential for anaphylaxis and the cost. It's about $2900-$5k per month. It's hideous, but thankfully, there are financial assistance options for most people. Between insurance and the financial assistance program, I have not had to pay a penny for treatment for the last year. Now I carry an EPIPen for my allergy shots and my Xolair appointments. I've never needed to use it.

This is a mistitled  video. None of the tips are actual tips to get rid of hives other than the #10 which is to just frickin wait for them to go away. Oh, thanks for the tip Doc. 5. Stay calm

8. Talk to your doc if the meds don't work

10. They'll eventually go away on their own for most people

comment_text:
I know exactly what the trigger for my hives is. My son died, and the hives popped out on both my arms, hand, and foot the instant I got the news. I was told that as my grief eased, my hives would go away. And they did, all except this one arm. It has been almost 6 years now, and it is still constant, day and night. Heat definitely is a trigger, but not always. That area has become drier than the rest of my body, although it never was before, so that is a trigger, too, if it gets too dry. I also have bronchitis, so warm showers are a must, but they are kept short. Stress can sometimes bring it on, but again, not always. Unless I am on the antihistamines and the creams, the special soapless soap, etc., I have hives as a constant. The only time I get relief is when my arm is greasy and slopped up, and I've popped pills morning and evening. 
Also, the hives were a constant, almost invisible bumps for 5 years, and then suddenly I developed a new kind, next to the regular ones , in an area of my arm that I had not had them before. Very visible indeed. They didn't stay too long and nor did the 3rd type of hives that popped up below my inner elbow area. But those nasty little almost invisible ones didn't ever leave. They have decided that I am their best friend and have moved in permanently, and I can't seem to disabuse them of the idea! I try not to itch but I often wake up in the middle of the night to find that I have itched my now burning arm raw in my sleep.
Is there any actual cure for this, or should I just accept that I am a pill popper for life? Some advice, please??? 🙏

comment_text: I don’t completely disagree with this video, but I work for a dermatologist and we see itching (pruritis) all the time that is mainly caused by liver problems/hepatitis C, drug eruption , and a big one....a symptom of cancer!... just to name a few. It could be as simple as a gallbladder issue, GERD, and all that jazz but always remember to still consult with your doctor so that labs can be at least drawn. If a patient just walks into a clinic and itching all over was their main symptom/chief complaint, you simply can not assume it’s their gallbladder/lack of bile salts (even if that is one of the causes). Blood work can tell you only half the story, but in this case it may tell you a lot of why that patient is itching all over. I have personally witnessed a patient come into clinic with just presenting with generalized pruritis, no medications, denies drug and alcohol use.....did a few simple blood tests and bam....patient had hep C and didn’t know it...you just never know and that’s why educating yourself with truthful information and questioning/presenting to your doctor about possible causes and treatment is still a must. But nonetheless Dr. Berg is a favorite of mine, but I just felt the need to make aware other very serious conditions can have a symptom such as pruritis. Much love!

What % of the population have life threatening dermatagraphia? I go into full blown anaphylaxis if mine is left untreated/unchecked.

 I was attacked by a swarm of mosquitoes, even while wearing repellent, and unknown to me at the time I had dermatagraphia. Naturally, I scratched all over and within 45 minutes I was in the ER.

comment_text:
Started intermittent fasting 6 months ago. IF is amazing if you haven't committed to it yet. But.... Hives appeared 2 weeks after starting. Figured it would go away since my body was detoxing from 5 years of junk food. Here we are with hives almost everyday!  From what I've been reading for months it's related to the gut health. I'm not a doctor, but it seems to disappear for those who do a strick diet change. Especially and most recently those who stick with a carnivore diet for NO LESS than 10-12 weeks. Helps reset your gut. I'm definitely going to try it starting next week. I Love my veggies but finding out most of my favs are high in histamine such as spinach and avocados. Yea it sucks!
I've only started this week keeping a food log. I'm also scheduled to see a natural path doc next month. 
This will be interesting.
Stay cool and don't scratch friends!

P.S. - Oatmeal baths are a God send!

comment_text:
I’ve been suffering from this as well for many years and finally started to try and find out the root cause and cure bc no medical professional can offer any advice beyond taking an allergy pill everyday. 

It seems to stem from an abundance of histamine in the body. That’s why an allergy pill will help but for me it was not enough. 
Histamine is produced in the body during an allergic reaction and there are many foods that naturally contain histamine. 

There are two primary enzymes in the body to break down histamine. One is DAO and the other HNMT. I found taking a soil based prebiotic and probiotic has helped my gut health which allowed my body to produce more of there enzymes necessary to break down the histamine in my body. 

This has seemed to cure my dermatographia

comment_text:
Sarans Hospital is the place where I realised ayurveda hospitals are expensive. I went here for urticaria, skin allergy, but did not had improvement.

I spent 15k on tests and medicines in the first two months but no progress. Then, they suggested taking high potential medicines costing 10k+ per month for the next three months or another option as 6k per month medicines for next 5 months. This made me question whether their main focus was on curing the issue or just reaching a round figure of an additional 30k. I stopped the treatment, as spending nearly 50k on a whole for skin allergy seemed excessive.

Allergies may not be 100% curable with medicines and we may need to identify the root cause but doctor posted a video on YouTube claiming that urticaria is 100% curable, which made me visit to the hospital. Unfortunately, I didn’t get the expected results. Please avoid making such videos if they do not reflect actual outcomes.

When I visited the first time, they forcefully added weight gain medicine for additional 2k without me asking, I'm not that under weight, I'm 69kg already.

When a patient comes in with a specific issue, the primary focus should be to address that problem first. Only after that is cured, any additional medications for other improvements be suggested. Otherwise, the patient ends up with added costs and unnecessary treatments without relief from their main issue.

There are many other things I don't find comfortable. For the first month, they gave medicines for 6k(4k+2k) with weight gain and let me know that, next month it will be just 4k without weight gain. However, in the second month, they added an extra medicine for 1k, bringing the total to 5k without any explanation. Their bill doesn't break down the cost of each individual medicine and is always a round figure like 4k, 5k, or 6k. (There is GST on top of this)

I suggest that patients ask about the individual medicines and understand what each one is for. 
Also, it may be better not to disclose your profession, such as working in IT, to avoid any chance of that influencing the cost of the prescribed treatment plan.

It's been six months since I stopped the treatment saying it's expensive but the assistant doctors keeps messaging on WhatsApp and calls for couriering the medicines for next course of treatment, which makes me wonder why they’re behaving more like sales representatives than medical professionals.

This is my experience with urticaria, skin allergy treatment at Sarans Hospital.

I stopped the treatment, as spending nearly 50k on a whole for skin allergy seemed excessive. Unfortunately, I didn’t get the expected results. Please avoid making such videos if they do not reflect actual outcomes. When I visited the first time, they forcefully added weight gain medicine for additional 2k without me asking, I'm not that under weight, I'm 69kg already. When a patient comes in with a specific issue, the primary focus should be to address that problem first. Only after that is cured, any additional medications for other improvements be suggested. Otherwise, the patient ends up with added costs and unnecessary treatments without relief from their main issue. There are many other things I don't find comfortable. For the first month, they gave medicines for 6k(4k+2k) with weight gain and let me know that, next month it will be just 4k without weight gain. Their bill doesn't break down the cost of each individual medicine and is always a round figure like 4k, 5k, or 6k. (There is GST on top of this) I suggest that patients ask about the individual medicines and understand what each one is for. Also, it may be better not to disclose your profession, such as working in IT, to avoid any chance of that influencing the cost of the prescribed treatment plan. comment_text: I had a severe case of CIU about 35 years ago, although no one ever gave it that name or any name for that matter.  (I'm 65 now).  It lasted 7 years.  I remember calling it my 7-year itch after it all ended.  I had all sorts of allergy testing - everything came back negative.  The wheals were huge with intense itching, burning; which included pain inside my chest.  They would appear on my arms, trunk and legs, but never my face.  This happened on almost a daily basis.  I knew when I was going to have an attack, because my chest would get very tight and painful.  Minutes later tons of hives would start to appear and grow.  They would last for hours.  It made me feel very sick.  I usually had to go to bed to be quiet in order to be calm and not scratch.  I always wore long sleeves and long pants no matter the season.  I ended up being transported to the Emergency Room several times with anaphylaxis and given epinephrine to open up my throat to breathe.  It was horrific.  No doctors ever found a cause.  I finally gave up searching for answers and just accepted it.  The condition showed up one day out of the blue, plagued me almost daily...and 7 years later disappeared just like that.  Not one attack since.  So strange, but I'm very grateful they left.  It actually messed with me psychologically also.  I did use biofeedback (meditation) to help cope.  Wouldn't wish this on my worst enemy...I hope they have found treatments for current suffers.

comment_text:
I started having urticaria in May 2021. At first, I didn’t pay much attention, but within a couple of months, the episodes became more frequent. I consulted several doctors and tried various medicines, but the problem kept returning once I stopped the medication. After about two years, I also developed angioedema, with swelling on my lips, eyelids, forehead, and chin. 

Among all medicines, levocetirizine helped me the most. Later, one doctor increased my dose gradually to 15 mg per day, which reduced my urticaria completely, though mild lip swelling occurred twice. After that, he advised me to continue 10 mg daily and take extra if any episode appeared. 

It’s now been around 50 days on 5 mg daily, and I haven’t had any urticaria or angioedema. I’ll stop it after two more weeks. You may also discuss this approach with your doctor.

comment_text: I had a mesh repair on a femoral hernia. 9 day's later I was covered in itchy burning rashes everywhere. Was so bad I was taken to hospital twice. Put on 6 steroids a day for one week and a antihistamine. Didn't help. I had second lot of steroids, still no change. Then my own doctor put me on fenofexodine, and a steroids cream. Also a menthol cream, and codeine pain killers. They definitely have helped but I am still getting savage flare ups globally. I saw dermatologist yesterday 28 July 2025 and showed him lots of photos plus haf 4 flare ups in front of him. He said I had chronic spontaneous urticaria,  and I need next level intervention,  so got to have the omalizumab injection twice in hospital as need to be watched for severe allergic reactions for 2 hours. Then it can be given at home once every 4 weeks. I do have many other chronic issues hypothyroidism, fibromyalgia, g.e.r.d, e.d.s, hypomobility, trapped nerves in c4 c7 in my neck, arthritis of the hips, I had a bowel resection,  gallbladder  removed, vertigo, chronic esophageal pain, and many other chronic issues like fatigue! So I will stay on fenofexodine and another antihistamines called loratadine to see if its any better. Also femotidine at night. Taking all these pills is giving me terrible oesophagus pain. It's literally detached me from reality, this urticaria has been life changing for me, im housebound with it, I need to be near my blocks of ice that I need at least 5 times a day or more. With fibromyalgia I can't stand the cold, so to be wrapped in ice packs is nothing short of torture, but it's the only quick way to stop the pain, the burning and the itch. I've had non stop back to back attacks since 18 june 25. I don't get any breaks longer than half hour. Some attacks I can cope with and deal with quickly, but the savage ones need hours and hours of needing ice packs. The savage attacks are what make you feel like your losing your mind. Anyway im hoping this injection will work and give me peace. Im 62 a great grandmother, I have very supportive children, I have great doctors trying to get everything under control, so im hopeful.

comment_text:
My dad suffered from cold urticaria.  He always told us he was allergic to the cold. 

It's a rare condition, but he told me one day doctors wanted to see him.  He was jetted from Colorado Springs to somewhere in upstate NY, spent an hour or two in the doctor's office, and then jetted back to Colorado Springs later that day.  I think it might have been one of the Mayo Clinics.  I know he was relieved that his condition was finally validated.  Someone somewhere was interested.

Recently, I discovered one of my students, an African American girl (I mention this only because there might be a demographic about African Americans or girls and urticaria) suffers from cold urticaria,  so I discussed my dad's situation with her.  I think she was relieved that I understood her condition and that I had had experience with it through my dad.  She's a lovely person, and she shouldn't have to suffer.

It's a rare condition, not life-threatening, but my dad must've just gritted his teeth when the weather got cold.  I remember the red marks on his legs.  He never mentioned itching, pain, or anything else, but dads have the obligation to be strong in front of their kids.  

Thank you, Vicki, for bringing attention to this disease.

comment_text: MORE videos on various kinds of angioedema Please. My experience: Chronic idiopathic angioedema -not inherited- Symptoms including various Combinations of upper and/or lip swelling, lip burning, lip tingling or a crawling sensation under the lip or around the mouth, blurred vision and very dry eye and lips AND-mouth , mouth ulcers and the feeling of back of tongue swelling  to a small degree. All of this can make it difficult to form word or enunciate. An irregular headache and brain fog is present. An internal heat that can only be calmed by showers or lying on cold tile floor. If it’s an intense flair up my bones may ache. I have had bone marrow test to rule out the bad drivers of the symptoms as well as being tested for lupus and related systemic issues. Lorazepam at /2 mg dose 3times a day mitigates the intensity of most of the symptoms. Treatment has included Allegra 24 hour 2times a day, nasocort 1 time each nostril am and pm, emergency inhaler as needed, time release prednisone in a 3 day 5 mg dose followed by a 3day 2mg dose. Sometimes a dose pace of Prednisone 40, 30, 20, 10mg and if that doesn’t work completely-repeating the 4day dosing. My symptoms do  go away completely. Occasionally they are so mild l sense normality. I am now familiar enough with my set of symptoms to know how to treat. Epidemiologists? Yes l do seek care and have been on one drug trial for this type of idiopathic angioedema- but couldn’t complete the trial due to muscle cramping. The drug is used for inherited angioedema transexenmic acid. Are any of the clinics finding any new trials? San Diego Clinic or the clinic in Germany? I am a mystery to most doctors. Gastro swelling and IBS-C seems to be related I’m told. I have this also l strictly follow the FOD-MAP guidelines. . It’s a day to day life. I have learned a lot but only know of one person similar to me. I go on living. I’m blessed to have been given faith. Tonight I’m treating myself with all of the above. Please write a comment if you know anyone like me.
